trajectory_summary: The call opens with a warm, motivated customer who has a specific solar vision and a detailed construction plan that signals high future load and long tenure. Parker exits the call at 01:53 after a single angle of resistance on ground-mount, then misses a second opening when Benjamin raises battery backup — a named Ion product. The call ends with Benjamin self-managing the exit and promising to call back in months. The energy leaked from the first objection and never recovered.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is 'unknown' — the correct classification is a missed opportunity / no appointment. This is not a DQ: Benjamin is a homeowner, actively planning a remodel, has an acre of land, is adding a large outbuilding, and explicitly raised battery backup interest. None of Ion's hard DQ rules apply. The call ended without an appointment because Parker conceded prematurely, not because the lead was unqualifiable.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Product catalog knowledge — Tesla Powerwall as Ion offering
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Oh, okay. But Okay. I was thinking batteries, you know, run run just so I have power at night or something or if some power goes out, I have power.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Parker directed a warm customer to competitors on a product Ion carries. Whether this is a knowledge gap (Parker doesn't know Ion sells Powerwall) or a scope error (Parker knows but didn't connect it), the outcome is the same: Ion lost a named-product conversation on a motivated lead. This is the highest-urgency fix because it is repeatable — any customer who raises battery backup will get the same referral-out response until Parker knows the catalog.

key_moments.quote: Unfortunately, we we actually don't anymore. We've kinda moved away from ground mounts just because of how expensive it it tends to be. You know, it's a lot more to to trench all the equipment into the ground and get the the equipment wired to the home and everything. It just kinda makes it less cost effective. So we wouldn't be able to help out with that.
key_moments.stewart_read: Parker's response to the ground-mount preference is a single-angle exit delivered with a cost-justification explanation that belongs to the closer tier. The strategic hypothesis — that Parker is deferring to set up a later move — is not supported: there is no probe of why Benjamin prefers ground mount, no curiosity created, and no bridge to any Ion product. The reactive read holds: Parker conceded immediately and explained the business rationale rather than asking a single question. The codex's multi_angle_resilience target is 3+ angles; Parker used one.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. Well, it was worth trying. Right? Yeah. Sorry about that. There's a few other companies that I'm sure you can reach out to that might be able to do.
key_moments.stewart_read: Parker explicitly closes the door and refers Benjamin to competitors after Benjamin's single 'I really don't wanna put it on the roof' line. The valid critique here is single-angle exit and failure to probe the roof-aversion reason before conceding — not anything about the construction timeline, which Benjamin had not yet shared. The strategic hypothesis — that Parker is clearing the call to focus on a better lead — is not supported: Benjamin is a warm, motivated homeowner with a construction project underway. The reactive read holds.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. Because I mean, we do more like residential. So our our kinda main aim is we we match or we we look at your electric bill so to see how much energy you would use, and then we we try to offset your bill with that many panels so we don't — I mean, anything more than that, like, you can get credits for your solar panels, but, the buyback program of, like, power isn't really what it used to be.
key_moments.stewart_read: Benjamin pushes back on the ground-mount denial with a logical argument about panel capacity. Parker responds with a net-metering explanation that is closer-tier content — bill offset methodology, buyback program mechanics, energy credits vs cash. This is scope creep in response to a customer question rather than a strategic choice. The explanation does not resolve Benjamin's concern (he wanted more panels, not a lesson on net metering) and consumes call time without advancing toward any Ion product.

key_moments.quote: Oh, okay. But Okay. I was thinking batteries, you know, run run just so I have power at night or something or if some power goes out, I have power.
key_moments.stewart_read: Benjamin explicitly raises battery backup as a use case — off-grid power at night and outage protection. This is a direct signal for the Tesla Powerwall, which the codex confirms is the only battery brand Ion carries and a named Ion product. Parker responds at 03:30 by telling Benjamin to 'find someone' — directing a warm customer to competitors on a product Ion sells. This is both a product knowledge failure (Parker may not know Ion carries Powerwall) and a cross-sell miss on a confirmed Ion offering. The strategic hypothesis — that Parker is deferring battery discussion to the closer — is not supported: Parker explicitly says 'find someone else,' not 'our specialist handles that.'
